prompt:
Security review of the gateway session store (sessions.py, attached, works fine today) came back with three findings, all now on my plate. Existing call sites construct `SessionStore(ttl_seconds, clock)` positionally and must keep working exactly as they do -- the EXISTING BEHAVIOR block in test_sessions.py already passes and has to stay green.

1. Signed cookie values. We currently put the raw session id in the cookie. New: construct with an optional keyword `secret` (bytes). `cookie_value(session_id)` returns `"<session_id>.<signature>"` where the signature is hex HMAC-SHA256 of the session id under the secret. `session_from_cookie(value)` reverses it: split on the FIRST dot, verify the signature with a constant-time comparison (hmac.compare_digest, not ==), and return the session id only if the signature checks out AND the session is still alive -- otherwise None, never an exception, no matter how mangled the value is. Both helpers raise RuntimeError if the store was built without a secret; that's a config error and I want it loud.

2. Rolling expiry. Today a session dies ttl seconds after create no matter what. New: every successful access -- get() or a valid session_from_cookie() -- pushes the expiry out to now + ttl (the injectable clock keeps this testable). A session idle for a full ttl still dies; untouched-since-create behavior is unchanged.

3. Concurrent-login cap. Optional keyword `max_sessions_per_user` (int). When create() would take a user past the cap, evict that user's LEAST RECENTLY ACTIVE session first (activity = create or any successful access from #2). Only LIVE sessions count against the cap -- destroyed and expired ones must not hold slots -- and the cap is strictly per user; other users' sessions are untouchable.

The rest of test_sessions.py pins all of this down: `python3 test_sessions.py` fully green is the bar. Test file is read-only. Stdlib only, as always.
